Just now

--

If you’ve spent any time building 3D experiences on the web, you’ve probably asked this question at some point: is Three.js still worth learning, or is it on its way out, replaced by newer, faster tech like raw WebGPU, Babylon.js, or something else entirely?
The short answer: Three.js isn’t just surviving in 2026 — it’s thriving, and it’s evolving fast enough to stay ahead of the technology that was supposed to make it obsolete.
Here’s the full picture.
The Big Shift: WebGL Is Being Replaced by WebGPU (And Three.js Is Riding the Wave)
For over a decade, WebGL was the only real option for 3D graphics in the browser. It worked, but it was built on aging foundations (OpenGL ES 2.0), and it had hard architectural limits — no proper compute shaders, poor multi-threading, and a rendering model that struggled once scenes got complex.
WebGPU was designed to fix all of that. But for years it stayed “almost ready,” mostly because Apple hadn’t shipped support in Safari. That changed in a big way: Safari 26 finally brought WebGPU support across macOS, iOS, iPadOS, and visionOS, which meant WebGPU support finally became close to universal across browsers.
